stem: What physical measurement provides contrast in phase-contrast MRA?

options.text: A T1 reduction caused by gadolinium
A velocity-dependent phase shift created by bipolar gradients
A proton-density difference between blood and tissue only
A frequency offset caused by fat shielding

explanation: Bipolar gradients cancel phase in stationary spins but leave a phase shift in moving spins. The magnitude and sign of that shift encode velocity along the selected direction.

stem: Why are flow-encoded and reference acquisitions compared in phase-contrast imaging?

options.text: Subtracting them creates longitudinal magnetization
The reference acquisition measures only T2 relaxation
Subtracting their phases removes background phase and isolates motion-related phase
The comparison replaces all spatial encoding

explanation: A reference and a velocity-encoded acquisition share many static phase offsets. Their phase difference emphasizes the component caused by moving spins.

stem: What does the selected velocity-encoding value represent?

options.text: The minimum velocity that can ever produce signal
The receiver bandwidth expressed in centimeters per second
The average tissue T1 within the vessel
The maximum unaliased velocity assigned to a phase shift of 180 degrees

explanation: VENC sets the relationship between velocity and phase. Velocities at or beyond the encoded range can wrap in phase, analogous to aliasing.

stem: What happens when VENC is set below the highest velocity in a vessel?

options.text: Velocity aliasing occurs
Slow-flow sensitivity disappears without aliasing
All stationary tissue becomes bright from inflow
Gadolinium concentration is underestimated

explanation: Velocities exceeding the encoded range cross the phase boundary and are displayed with an incorrect magnitude or direction. Raising VENC removes the wrap but reduces sensitivity to slow flow.

stem: What is the disadvantage of setting VENC much higher than the velocities of interest?

options.text: Fast flow aliases more severely
Small velocity differences produce very small phase shifts and are measured less sensitively
Stationary tissue acquires a 180-degree phase shift
The sequence becomes contrast-enhanced automatically

explanation: High VENC reserves the phase range for high speeds, compressing slow velocities into a narrow phase interval. Noise then has a larger effect on low-flow measurement.

stem: What capability distinguishes phase-contrast MRA from basic TOF MRA?

options.text: It requires no gradients
It images only stationary tissue
It can quantify velocity and flow direction
It cannot acquire magnitude information

explanation: The velocity-dependent phase signal can be calibrated to measure speed, direction, and volumetric flow. TOF primarily exploits inflow enhancement and is usually qualitative.
